html:
KC Co. (KRX:029460)

KC is not the old “CMP-cleaning monopoly” story in the prior draft. The better thesis is duller and stronger: KC is a Korean semiconductor fab utility, gas and chemical infrastructure, and holding-company asset play sitting directly behind the Samsung and SK Hynix AI memory build-out, while still trading at a deep asset and earnings discount.

At the May 18, 2026 close of 36,200 KRW, KC’s market value is roughly 0.35 to 0.40 trillion KRW depending on treasury-share treatment. The stock is already up sharply, but it still trades around 0.45x to 0.50x book, about 9x trailing earnings, and roughly 3x to 4x EBITDA after cash and securities. This is not optically cheap because of a collapsing business. KC just filed Q1 2026 revenue of 212.5 billion KRW, operating profit of 14.0 billion KRW, and net profit of 26.0 billion KRW, a clear rebound from Q4 2025’s depressed 3.6 billion KRW operating profit.

The mispricing is that the market still sees KC as a lumpy, low-margin fab construction and equipment vendor. The company’s own site describes the current parent as focused on specialty gas supply systems and chemical supply systems, including BSGS, gas cabinets, VMB, gas mixers, CCSS, and CDS. That sounds mundane until Korean HBM capacity becomes the bottleneck. New HBM and advanced DRAM lines do not just need EUV tools and TC bonders. They need ultra-clean gas delivery, chemical delivery, hook-up, scrubbers, pipework, control systems, and locally qualified suppliers that can execute inside Samsung and SK Hynix fabs.

The forward setup is unusually good because the customer capex cycle has changed from “memory recovery” to “AI memory shortage.” SK Hynix said in its official Q1 2026 release that customer demand exceeds supply capacity and that 2026 investment will rise significantly, focused on M15X ramp-up, Yongin infrastructure, and EUV. Samsung has also been explicit that memory supply remains short and that the 2027 demand gap could worsen, with Reuters reporting Samsung is on track to more than triple HBM revenue in 2026 and has begun HBM4 sales for Nvidia’s Vera Rubin platform. KC is not the glamorous HBM component supplier, which is exactly why the stock remains mispriced. It is the infrastructure layer that gets ordered when fabs move from PowerPoint to tools-in.

The capital allocation signal is the second part of the thesis. KC is no longer merely sitting on cash. In February 2026 it announced a 361,116-share cancellation worth about 12 billion KRW, then in March it announced another 1,849,612-share cancellation worth about 57 billion KRW. Together, that is about 69 billion KRW of treasury retirement, enormous relative to a roughly 0.4 trillion KRW equity value. The company also raised the dividend to 430 KRW per share. This matters because the classic Korean holdco discount becomes less punitive when excess capital is actually retired rather than trapped.

The balance sheet is the third support. At 2025 year-end, KC had 237.4 billion KRW of cash and short-term investments against 44.0 billion KRW of debt, or 193.4 billion KRW of net cash. It also carried large long-term investments and associates. Book value per share was about 77,000 KRW on vendor data, more than double the current price. The caveat is important: 2025 free cash flow was only about 1.1 billion KRW because capex and working capital absorbed cash, so this is not an EV/FCF screen. The right valuation lens is forward earnings power plus asset backing plus capital return.

My base-case 2026 build is 840 to 900 billion KRW of revenue, 60 to 70 billion KRW of EBIT, and 55 to 65 billion KRW of net profit attributable to common shareholders, helped by equity income and a lower share count. That implies roughly 5,000 to 6,000 KRW of forward EPS. At 36,200 KRW, the stock is on roughly 6x to 7x forward earnings and below half of book, despite a balance sheet that can keep shrinking the float.

The best upside case is not that KC becomes a high-multiple global equipment name. The best case is simpler: the market stops treating it like a sleepy construction vendor and starts valuing it as a scarce Korean fab infrastructure supplier with assets, earnings, and buybacks. There is also no crowded sell-side ownership here. Company Guide shows no recent three-month broker target, while liquidity remains modest and the float is tight. That is the kind of ignored setup where a few quarters of clean execution can move the multiple.

Risks are real. Q1 operating profit was down year over year against a strong Q1 2025, so the inflection is not linear. Construction and hook-up revenue is lumpy, and margins can compress when project mix turns unfavorable. Samsung and SK Hynix capex timing is the core driver. If HBM demand pauses, fabs slip, or Korean customers delay utility orders, KC can look cheap for a long time. The holding-company discount may also persist if management stops cancelling shares. But the downside is cushioned by cash, asset value, a profitable core business, and a now-visible willingness to retire stock.

Bear target: 28,000 KRW, down 23%. HBM capex timing slips, 2026 EBIT falls toward 35 to 40 billion KRW, FCF remains weak, and the market values KC at only 0.35x to 0.40x book.

Base target: 58,000 KRW, up 60%. KC earns 5,500 to 6,000 KRW of forward EPS, book value remains around 77,000 to 80,000 KRW per share, and the stock rerates to 0.75x book or about 9.5x earnings.

Bull target: 85,000 KRW, up 135%. Korean HBM infrastructure orders stay strong into 2027, EBIT reaches 80 to 90 billion KRW, EPS approaches 7,000 KRW, and the market values KC closer to 1.1x book or 12x earnings.
